relevant_files.rationale: The CLI->Server boundary: translates CLI args (port, host, target, debug) into startHttpServer() options. Owns security gate that silently downgrades non-loopback host to 127.0.0.1 when no auth token is provided.
relevant_files.topic_relation: Entry point of the full interaction chain — this file is where the CLI layer hands off to the server layer and establishes the entire component lifecycle.

key_code.description: Reads CLI args, validates host/port, reads FABRIC_AUTH_TOKEN from env, calls startHttpServer() — tight coupling: workspaceRoot = process.cwd() baked in here
Security guard: non-loopback host silently falls back to 127.0.0.1 if no auth token; logs a warning but continues — potential footgun if users miss the warning
Auth token sourced exclusively from FABRIC_AUTH_TOKEN env var — no CLI flag alternative

relevant_files.rationale: Dual-transport MCP factory: createFabricServer() registers all 4 tools; startStdioServer() and startHttpServer() expose the two transport modes. This file is the central bifurcation point between stdio (agent direct) and HTTP (agent + dashboard) operation.
relevant_files.topic_relation: Core architectural boundary: the server package's public interface. How each transport mode is initialized and what is shared (tool registrations) vs per-transport (session, process context) is defined here.

key_code.description: Creates McpServer and registers 4 tools synchronously. Called once for stdio, called per-session for HTTP. No dependency injection — all tool handlers resolve projectRoot from process.env at call time.
Dynamic import of ./http.js to avoid loading Express when only stdio is needed. Accepts dashboardDistPath and dev options, not surfaced to CLI serve command — extension seam only partially open.
Allows file to be used both as a library (createFabricServer) and as a standalone stdio server binary — dual entry point pattern.

relevant_files.rationale: The entire HTTP layer architecture: session-per-connection McpServer pattern, JsonlEventStore for MCP event replay, bearer auth scoping, and the flat REST API surface for the dashboard all live here.
relevant_files.topic_relation: Most architecturally dense file in the system — contains three distinct coupling concerns: MCP session lifecycle, persistent event replay store, and REST API registration. All four coupling risks identified in this analysis originate here.

key_code.description: Side-effects: sets process.env.FABRIC_PROJECT_ROOT (L128) — global process state mutation that breaks concurrent server instances. Creates one Map<sessionId, FabricHttpSession> in closure scope. Bearer auth selectively applied to /api and /events, not /mcp.

Called per MCP initialize request. Dynamic imports createFabricServer() creating a new McpServer (and re-registering all 4 tools) for every connected agent. onsessionclosed cleans up Map but transport.onclose is also bound as a redundant cleanup guard.

Append-only JSONL file as MCP event store for StreamableHTTPServerTransport replay. replayEventsAfter() does a full file scan every time — O(n) on file size with no upper bound. Shares the same ledger file path with intent entries; distinguishes them by checking kind !== 'mcp-event'.

relevant_files.rationale: resolveProjectRoot() reads process.env.FABRIC_PROJECT_ROOT — the implicit context injection mechanism used by all 4 MCP tool handlers. This is the primary coupling between HTTP server startup and MCP tool execution.
relevant_files.topic_relation: Architectural smell: MCP tools have no explicit context parameter; they obtain projectRoot through process.env at call time. This design breaks the explicit-dependency principle and makes the server non-reentrant.

key_code.description: Returns process.env.FABRIC_PROJECT_ROOT ?? process.cwd(). MCP tools call this inside each tool handler — not at registration time. This means the env var must be set before any tool is invoked.

Synchronous readFileSync — blocking I/O in an async server. Used in REST API handlers and MCP tool handlers. The synchronous read is safe for small files but contradicts the async design of surrounding code.

relevant_files.rationale: The Server->Dashboard SSE boundary. createEventsHandler() implements file-watching push for 4 state files (.fabric/agents.meta.json, .fabric/human-lock.json, .fabric/forensic.json, .intent-ledger.jsonl), debounced at 75ms, with a 10-client connection limit.
relevant_files.topic_relation: Full SSE architecture including: shared-watcher lazy-start/stop lifecycle, per-event-type discriminated publishing, stateful ledger offset tracking (append-only reads), and human-lock drift detection via sha256 content hashing.

key_code.description: Returns a closure over shared EventsState (clients Set, watcher, ledgerOffset, humanLockSnapshot). Lazy watcher start on first SSE connect; watcher stop when last client disconnects. No Last-Event-ID replay — missed events during disconnect are permanently lost.

Validates every outbound event through fabricEventSchema.parse() before broadcast. Dead clients detected by writableEnded check or write() exception. Runs fabricEventSchema Zod parse on every broadcast — overhead proportional to event frequency.

Incremental ledger read: tracks byte offset in state.ledgerOffset, reads only new bytes on each change. Handles truncation by resetting offset to 0. Parses only LedgerEntry records, skips kind='mcp-event' lines — the shared file has two record kinds.

relevant_files.rationale: Defines the FabricEvent discriminated union (5 event types) as the shared contract between server SSE push and dashboard event consumption. The z.discriminatedUnion ensures exhaustive type handling on both ends.
relevant_files.topic_relation: The shared type package acts as the schema contract for the entire SSE channel — any new event type must be added here before it can be sent or received by any component.

key_code.description: Union of MetaUpdatedEvent | LockDriftEvent | LockApprovedEvent | LedgerAppendedEvent | DriftDetectedEvent. The type field is the discriminator.
z.discriminatedUnion('type', [...]) — used by broadcastEvent() to validate before send. Dashboard's parseFabricEvent() does a manual check rather than using this schema — asymmetric validation.

relevant_files.rationale: Defines AgentsMeta schema and the layer/topology derivation logic (L0/L1/L2, mirror/cross-cutting). Contains z.preprocess for default field derivation — this schema is parsed on every fab_get_rules and fab_plan_context call.
relevant_files.topic_relation: The agents meta schema is the data backbone for the entire rule-delivery system. The structural derivation logic embedded in the schema (layer from file path depth, topology from _cross segment) means renaming conventions would silently break rule classification.

key_code.description: z.preprocess wraps the base schema to inject derived defaults for layer and topology_type. This makes persisted files forward-compatible when new fields are added.
Layer derived from file path: AGENTS.md=L0, _cross segment=L1, directory depth<=2=L1, else L2. Convention-over-config approach — path naming drives behavior.

relevant_files.rationale: Centralizes Zod schemas for all REST API request validation (ledger query, history state, human-lock approve, intent annotate). Both server (input validation) and dashboard client.ts define request types — partial coupling because dashboard defines its own TypeScript types instead of sharing these schemas.
relevant_files.topic_relation: Architectural gap: server validates with ledgerQuerySchema/humanLockApproveRequestSchema from shared, but dashboard/src/api/client.ts defines parallel TS types (LedgerQuery, ApproveHumanLockBody) rather than importing from shared — two sources of truth for the same API surface.

key_code.description: Server-side validation schema. Dashboard's ApproveHumanLockBody type is a parallel TS interface, not derived from this schema — they can diverge silently.

relevant_files.rationale: The MCP host auto-detection layer: resolveClients() detects 7 AI host types by filesystem probe (existsSync on known config paths/directories). Returns ClientConfigWriter[] for each detected host — addIfDetected() controls conditional inclusion.
relevant_files.topic_relation: CLI->MCP Host boundary: this file writes the JSON config entries that tell AI hosts how to connect to the fabric server. The detection logic is purely filesystem-based with no runtime confirmation that the host is actually installed or running.

key_code.description: Checks 7 hosts: ClaudeCodeCLI (~/.claude OR ./.claude), ClaudeCodeDesktop (OS-specific path), Cursor (./.cursor), Windsurf (./.windsurf), RooCode (./.roo), GeminiCLI (~/.gemini OR GEMINI.md), CodexCLI (~/.codex). Falls back to workspace-level probe for most. No detection for VS Code Copilot or Continue.dev.
configuredPath always overrides detection. If configuredPath present, adds writer regardless of whether host is detected. Explicit override bypasses all auto-detection.

relevant_files.rationale: Implements JSON-based MCP config writing for 5 host types (ClaudeCodeCLI, Cursor, Windsurf, RooCode, GeminiCLI). The abstract JsonClientConfigWriter class defines the detect/write contract. ClaudeCodeCLI writes to project-level .claude/settings.json while detection checks ~/.
relevant_files.topic_relation: Architectural split in ClaudeCodeCLI: detection checks global ~/.claude dir (confirms Claude is installed) but writes to project-level .claude/settings.json (scopes MCP to project). This is intentional but creates a split signal — detection tells you 'Claude exists', write location tells you 'scope to project'.

key_code.description: Merges fabric entry into existing mcpServers object under key 'fabric'. Preserves other server entries. Writes with 2-space JSON formatting + trailing newline.
Checks both ~/.claude AND ./.claude for existence (either triggers write). Writes to .claude/settings.json in project root — not the global config.

relevant_files.rationale: Defines the primary Agent->Server MCP tool boundary. fab_get_rules is the agent's entry gate for Fabric rules. The MANDATORY keyword in description is the behavioral contract with the LLM.
relevant_files.topic_relation: The MCP tool->service->filesystem call chain: tool handler -> getRules() service -> readAgentsMeta() + minimatch glob matching + file reads. No caching at any layer — every tool call re-reads all matched rule files.

key_code.description: Registers fab_get_rules tool. Tool handler calls resolveProjectRoot() (process.env) then delegates to getRules() service. No request context threading — projectRoot is ambient.
Optional stale-detection field: agent sends prior revision hash; server returns stale:true if hash doesn't match current revision. Cache invalidation at the protocol level.

relevant_files.rationale: fab_append_intent closes the agent->ledger write loop. The inputSchema imports aiLedgerEntrySchema from shared (omitting id/source/ts) — the schema is the interface contract between agent and server.
relevant_files.topic_relation: Data-flow anchor: after agent completes a task, this tool is called to persist the intent entry to .intent-ledger.jsonl. This write is what triggers the SSE ledger:appended event to the dashboard — completing the full agent->dashboard notification chain.

key_code.description: MANDATORY description ensures LLM calls after task completion. Delegates to appendIntent() service which also triggers audit log events (best-effort, never blocks intent recording).

relevant_files.rationale: The complete dashboard->server REST API client. Defines 8 REST endpoints + 1 SSE endpoint consumed by the dashboard. Also defines parallel TypeScript types for API responses — many of which duplicate types from @fenglimg/fabric-shared.
relevant_files.topic_relation: Architecture coupling point: dashboard uses relative URLs (/api/..., /events) which only work when dashboard is served from the same origin as the server — no CORS configuration, no configurable base URL. Hard-coded to co-hosting assumption.

key_code.description: Manual type check: typeof parsed.type === 'string' && 'payload' in parsed. Does NOT use fabricEventSchema from shared — asymmetric validation; server validates outbound but dashboard does not validate inbound.

Returns new EventSource('/events') — relative URL, no auth header possible with native EventSource API. If FABRIC_AUTH_TOKEN is set on server, the /events SSE endpoint is protected but EventSource cannot send Authorization header — potential auth bypass gap.
Locally defined TypeScript interfaces duplicating what server's doctor service returns. Not imported from shared package — two divergence risks.

relevant_files.rationale: Dashboard-side SSE consumption: Preact hook using signals for global event state. Subscribes to all 5 FabricEvent types. Event history capped at 50 entries in memory.
relevant_files.topic_relation: The final hop in the server->dashboard push chain. Architectural note: signals (fabricEventsSignal, fabricConnectedSignal) escape component scope — events are globally accessible across the dashboard. Wires reconnect behavior but loses events during disconnection.

key_code.description: Creates new EventSource on mount, registers typed event listeners for each FabricEvent type. Cleans up listeners on unmount. Does not pass Last-Event-ID on reconnect — all events between disconnect and reconnect are lost.
Global signal holding last 50 events. Using Preact signals means any component can subscribe to the full event history without prop drilling — appropriate for dashboard-wide event state.

relevant_files.rationale: timingSafeEqual-based bearer auth middleware. SHA-256 digests both expected and provided tokens before comparison to prevent timing attacks. Applied only to /api and /events routes, not /mcp — asymmetric auth surface.
relevant_files.topic_relation: Security architecture: /mcp endpoint has no bearer auth applied (createFabricHttpApp L131-L135). MCP connections are unauthenticated even when FABRIC_AUTH_TOKEN is set. The SSE /events endpoint is protected but native EventSource cannot send Authorization headers.

key_code.description: Middleware factory. hashToken() pre-computes expected digest at creation time. timingSafeEqual prevents timing-oracle attacks. Returns 401 with UNAUTHORIZED code on failure.

Auth applied to /api/* and /events, explicitly NOT applied to /mcp — MCP is unauthenticated. This is likely intentional (MCP hosts manage their own auth via stdio/local transport) but creates an unauthenticated HTTP entry point.

relevant_files.rationale: Core AgentsMeta and AgentsMetaNode TypeScript interfaces. Defines the data model that flows through every layer: written by CLI sync-meta, read by MCP tools, published via SSE, consumed by dashboard.
relevant_files.topic_relation: Single-source-of-truth for the agents registry data model across all 4 packages. Any schema evolution (adding fields, changing types) propagates through all layers.

key_code.description: file, scope_glob, deps, priority, layer, topology_type, hash. The scope_glob field is what fab_get_rules uses for minimatch path matching — glob pattern determines which rules apply to which file.

relevant_files.rationale: Dashboard static asset serving: registerDashboardStatic() uses the DEFAULT_STATIC_DIR relative to server package's dist, with a catch-all SPA route regex that excludes /api/, /mcp/, and /events/ prefixes.
relevant_files.topic_relation: Co-hosting architecture: dashboard is served from the same Express app as the API. The regex /^/(?!api(?:/|$)|mcp(?:/|$)|events(?:/|$)).*/ is the routing boundary between SPA and API.

key_code.description: If dev mode, does not serve static (assumes Vite proxy). Otherwise expects dashboard dist at DEFAULT_STATIC_DIR (relative to server dist). SPA fallback route excludes /api, /mcp, /events paths from catch-all.

key_findings.title: CLI->Server boundary is clean but under-parameterized
key_findings.description: serve.ts correctly delegates to startHttpServer() with 4 parameters (port, projectRoot, host, authToken). The public options interface in server/src/index.ts includes dashboardDistPath and dev fields, but these are not exposed to the CLI serve command — they exist only for programmatic use. The boundary is clean but the CLI surface is smaller than the server API surface.
key_findings.recommendation: No action needed unless dashboard path override becomes a use case.

key_findings.title: process.env mutation for projectRoot breaks multi-instance safety
key_findings.description: createFabricHttpApp() (http.ts L128) sets process.env.FABRIC_PROJECT_ROOT = projectRoot. All 4 MCP tool handlers call resolveProjectRoot() which reads this env var. This is global process state — if two server instances were ever created in the same process (test suites, programmatic embedding), the second would overwrite the first's project root. The design also violates explicit dependency principles: tool handlers have an invisible ambient dependency rather than an injected context.
key_findings.recommendation: Pass projectRoot via tool call context or McpServer metadata instead of process.env. Alternatively, bind tool handlers at registration time with projectRoot in closure scope.

key_findings.title: Session-per-connection McpServer creates O(n) tool registrations
key_findings.description: Every MCP initialize request creates a new McpServer and re-registers all 4 tools via createFabricServer() (http.ts L176). Since tools are stateless (projectRoot comes from env, not session state), there is no functional reason for per-session server instances — this is an over-allocation. A single shared McpServer could serve all sessions if projectRoot were injected per-request rather than per-server.
key_findings.recommendation: Create McpServer once, inject per-request context via StreamableHTTPServerTransport's request metadata or closure binding.

key_findings.title: JsonlEventStore O(n) full-scan on every replay with shared ledger file
key_findings.description: JsonlEventStore.replayEventsAfter() reads the entire JSONL file to find the starting event ID. The ledger file also contains non-MCP records (intent entries with kind != 'mcp-event'). As the server runs, this file grows unboundedly and every MCP reconnect triggers a full scan. Additionally, parseLedgerAppendedEvents() uses the same file for SSE events with an independent byte-offset tracker.
key_findings.recommendation: Separate MCP event store from the intent ledger file. Cap the MCP event store with a fixed-size ring buffer (last N events) or use an in-memory store with optional persistence.

key_findings.title: SSE /events has no Last-Event-ID replay — events lost during reconnect
key_findings.description: The server's EventsState tracks ledgerOffset for incremental reads but does not implement the SSE Last-Event-ID reconnect protocol. Events generated between a disconnect and reconnect are permanently lost. The dashboard's useEvents hook creates a new EventSource which will miss all events that occurred during the gap. The EventSource browser API supports automatic reconnect with Last-Event-ID, but the server does not honor this header.
key_findings.recommendation: Buffer the last N events per client in EventsState and replay events with IDs after Last-Event-ID on reconnect. Alternatively maintain a small server-side ring buffer shared across all clients.

key_findings.title: Dashboard defines parallel type definitions instead of importing from shared
key_findings.description: packages/dashboard/src/api/client.ts defines DoctorReport, ScanReport, HumanLockStatus, ApproveHumanLockBody, AnnotateIntentBody and others as local TypeScript interfaces. These overlap with types available from @fenglimg/fabric-shared. parseFabricEvent() does a manual type check instead of using fabricEventSchema from shared. This creates two sources of truth and allows the dashboard types to silently drift from server definitions.
key_findings.recommendation: Import and re-export server-defined types from @fenglimg/fabric-shared in the dashboard. Use fabricEventSchema.safeParse() in parseFabricEvent() for symmetric validation.

key_findings.title: /mcp endpoint is unauthenticated even when FABRIC_AUTH_TOKEN is set
key_findings.description: Bearer auth middleware is applied only to app.use('/api', bearerAuth) and app.use('/events', bearerAuth). The /mcp endpoint has no auth guard. Any client that can reach the server can establish an MCP session and call all 4 tools (read rules, append ledger entries, update registry, plan context). Combined with the host defaulting to 127.0.0.1, this is only safe on loopback. Any remote deployment without port-level firewall exposes unauthenticated MCP.
key_findings.recommendation: Apply bearer auth to /mcp if FABRIC_AUTH_TOKEN is set, or document explicitly that /mcp is intentionally unauthenticated (relying on network isolation). The /events auth gap (EventSource cannot send Authorization header) also needs resolution — either query-param token or cookie.

key_findings.title: MCP tool API surface: text-only responses, no outputSchema, no resources primitive
key_findings.description: All 4 MCP tools use createTextResponse(JSON.stringify(payload)) — returning structured data as a JSON string inside a text content block. MCP 2025-06-18 supports outputSchema for structuredContent responses. Additionally, AGENTS.md and rule files are re-read from disk on every fab_get_rules and fab_plan_context call with no caching. The MCP resources primitive (with subscribe:true) could eliminate this redundant I/O and allow agent-side caching.
key_findings.recommendation: Add outputSchema to tool definitions. Expose AGENTS.md and rule files as MCP resources with subscribe:true + notifications/resources/updated on sync-meta.

key_findings.title: @fenglimg/fabric-shared is a well-designed single source of truth — with one gap
key_findings.description: The shared package exports types, Zod schemas, i18n, and API contracts used by CLI, server, and dashboard. The discriminated union FabricEvent schema and agentsMetaSchema with z.preprocess are strong design choices. The one gap: dashboard/src/api/client.ts does not import REST request/response types from shared, creating a parallel type world for the HTTP API surface.
key_findings.recommendation: Add REST response type exports to shared package. The dashboard should consume these rather than defining local interfaces.

key_findings.title: Dashboard co-hosting assumption creates a hard architectural constraint
key_findings.description: The dashboard uses relative URLs (/api/*, /events) and native EventSource with no configurable base URL or auth header. This means the dashboard can only work when served from the same origin as the server. The static serving (registerDashboardStatic) and the dashboard's fetch calls are tightly coupled to the single-origin deployment model.
key_findings.recommendation: If standalone dashboard deployment becomes a use case, a configurable base URL and token injection mechanism would be needed. For current scope, co-hosting is the correct constraint.

code_anchors.significance: Three architectural decisions in 20 lines: (1) global env mutation for projectRoot (breaks multi-instance), (2) auth applied to /api+/events but not /mcp (asymmetric security), (3) sessions Map in closure = per-app session isolation (correct for single instance).

enableJsonResponse: true disables SSE streaming for MCP transport — long-running tools cannot emit progress. Each session allocates a new McpServer. Dual cleanup (onsessionclosed + transport.onclose) is defensive but redundant.

Synchronous readFileSync in an async Express server — acceptable for small meta files but architecturally inconsistent. resolveProjectRoot() is the implicit context injection mechanism — tools have no explicit dependency on projectRoot.

Lazy watcher lifecycle (start on first connect, stop on last disconnect) is resource-efficient. Hard limit of 10 SSE clients. Missing: Last-Event-ID header processing for replay on reconnect — any events generated during a client gap are permanently lost.

Two gaps: (1) native EventSource cannot send Authorization header — when auth is enabled, dashboard SSE breaks silently; (2) parseFabricEvent does minimal validation, skipping the Zod schema from shared — server validates outbound but client does not validate inbound, asymmetric trust.

Every fab_get_rules call: reads agents.meta.json (sync), AGENTS.md (async), all human-lock entries, then each matched rule file. No caching layer. For a project with 50 rule files, every agent tool call triggers 50+ file reads. The client_hash stale detection is the only optimization — if hash matches, you could short-circuit but currently don't.

Detection is purely filesystem-based (existsSync). No runtime probe that host is installed or running. Adding a new MCP host type requires: (1) new existsSync probe here, (2) new writer class, (3) new ClientKind type. Extension requires modifying resolver.ts — not open-closed.

chain: serve.ts: startHttpServer({ port, projectRoot=cwd(), host, authToken })
http.ts: createFabricHttpApp() — sets process.env.FABRIC_PROJECT_ROOT, registers REST API routes, registers /mcp handler, registers /events SSE handler

call_chains.entry: MCP Host (e.g. Claude Code) sends initialize request to POST /mcp
chain: http.ts: createSession() — imports createFabricServer(), creates McpServer, registers 4 tools, creates StreamableHTTPServerTransport
server/index.ts: createFabricServer() — registers get-rules, plan-context, append-intent, update-registry
Response: 200 with session ID in MCP response
services/get-rules.ts: loadGetRulesContext() → readAgentsMeta() [sync readFileSync] + readFile(AGENTS.md) + readHumanLock()
services/get-rules.ts: resolveRulesForPath() → minimatch each node.scope_glob against path
matched nodes sorted by priority → parallel readFile() for each matched rule file
return { revision_hash, stale: client_hash !== revision, rules: { L0, L1[], L2[], human_locked_nearby[] } }

call_chains.entry: Agent calls fab_append_intent({ entry: { intent, affected_paths, ... } })
chain: api/events.ts: broadcastEvent() → SSE frame to all connected dashboard clients
GET / → api/static.ts: express.static serves dashboard dist
Initial data fetch: client.getRules() → GET /api/rules → readAgentsMeta()
UI renders initial state; SSE events update state reactively
resolver.ts: 7x existsSync probes for host detection
For each detected host: writer.write(serverPath, workspaceRoot)
json.ts: writeJsonClientConfig() → readJsonConfig() + merge fabric entry + writeFile()
Result: host config file updated with { mcpServers: { fabric: { command, args } } }
MCP Host auto-detects config change → server connection available on next agent session

questions_for_user.question: Is /mcp being unauthenticated intentional? The current design applies FABRIC_AUTH_TOKEN only to /api and /events, leaving the MCP endpoint open. If the server is ever exposed beyond loopback (e.g. in a Docker container or team environment), any client can call all 4 MCP tools without auth.

Is the session-per-McpServer design intentional, or was a shared server considered? Each MCP initialize request creates a full new McpServer with 4 tool registrations. If the reason is session isolation (e.g. per-session tool overrides in the future), it's worth the cost. If it was the easiest implementation, a shared server with per-request context would be more efficient.

Are there plans to support remote dashboard access (i.e. dashboard on a different origin than the server)? The current architecture hardcodes relative URLs and uses native EventSource which cannot send auth headers — both would need to change for any remote scenario.

Is the .intent-ledger.jsonl file intentionally shared between MCP event replay (JsonlEventStore) and intent ledger entries, or was it an implementation convenience? The two record types in the same file (kind='mcp-event' vs ledger entries) creates coupling between MCP session infrastructure and business data.

Is there a plan to address SSE event loss on reconnect? Currently, if the browser tab reconnects after a network blip, any events generated during the disconnect window are lost (no Last-Event-ID replay). For the human-lock drift and ledger-appended events, this could cause the dashboard to show stale state until a manual refresh.
